title: Chiral Quantum Entanglement Transfer with Giant Atoms
authors: Peng-Fei Wang, Lei Huang, Yi-Long-Yue Guo, Jing Wang, Han-Xiao Zhang, Hong Yang, Dong Yan
abstract: We investigate entanglement transfer in a multi-giant-atom waveguide system. By tailoring chiral spontaneous emission and exploiting dark-state dynamics, the setup enables perfect, unidirectional sequential or selective transfer of quantum states and their associated entanglement. The distance between two entangled atoms, i.e., the entanglement length, can be dynamically adjusted, allowing robust conversion between long-range and short-range entanglement during propagation. The system inherently converges to a dark state, guaranteeing high-fidelity directional transfer. When the additional phase is modulated as a periodic piecewise function, spatially separated giant atoms exhibit stable, nearly lossless state exchange and maintain steady entanglement even under non-Markovian conditions. This behavior mimics conventional braided architectures without suffering from propagation delays or spatial restrictions. Our proposal offers a scalable pathway for continuous long-distance entanglement transport and resilient state exchange in quantum networks.
summary: The paper proposes using an array of giant atoms coupled to a waveguide to realize perfect and unidirectional transfer of quantum states and entanglement. By controlling chiral spontaneous emission and dark-state dynamics through phase modulation, the system achieves robust state exchange even under non-Markovian conditions. This offers a scalable platform for long-distance quantum networking.
main_problem: The research aims to achieve perfect, unidirectional transfer of quantum states and associated entanglement across a multi-giant-atom waveguide system.
main_result: By engineering chiral spontaneous emission and exploiting dark-state dynamics, the setup enables robust, lossless state exchange capable of mimicking braided architectures without structural limitations.
method: The analysis involves deriving equations of motion for the single-excitation subspace using a total Hamiltonian ($H = H_a + H_w + H_{aw}$) and applying phase-matching conditions to control directional coupling.
model_or_system: The system consists of an array of N identical two-level giant atoms coupled to an infinite one-dimensional waveguide lattice. The dynamics are governed by time-dependent coupling coefficients $g_l(t)$ and engineered phases $\phi_l$.
key_observables: Fidelity of state transfer ($F(t)$), maximum interatomic concurrence ($C_{\max}$), population probabilities ($P_l(t)$), and entanglement length.
important_parameters: The additional phase ($\phi_l$), coupling coefficients ($g_l(t)$), and the ratio of decay rates ($\kappa_a/\kappa_0$).
main_assumptions: Analysis is performed in the single-excitation subspace, utilizing chiral spontaneous emission and assuming specific boundary conditions for perfect transfer.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate schematics of the multi-atom structure, time evolution of field intensity and population, and plots showing state/entanglement transfer dynamics under varying dissipation regimes.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the Hamiltonian and system setup to deriving equations of motion. It then details using phase-matching conditions and time-reversed couplings for perfect state transfer, culminating in demonstrating periodic, lossless exchange via piecewise phase modulation.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses quantum network protocols by proposing a physical mechanism (chiral coupling) to achieve high-fidelity, controllable entanglement transport over long distances, which is central to quantum information theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of a discrete multi-giant-atom structure coupled to an infinite one-dimensional lattice. Each two-level giant atom labeled l has transition frequency ωa. The l-th atom couples to the lattice sites n2l−1 and n2l with time- dependent coefficient gl(t). The atom size ds = n2l −n2l−1 and the interatomic distance dw = n2l+1 −n2l are uniform across the array. An additional phase difference φl between the two coupling points of each atom enables control of in- terference effects such as chiral emission and dark-state engi- neering.

Figure 1 shows the system: an array of N identical two- level giant atoms with transition frequency ωa coupled to an infinite one-dimensional lattice. Each lattice site has resonance frequency ω0, and η denotes the nearest- neighbor hopping rate. The l-th atom couples to the lattice sites n2l−1 and n2l with the time-dependent cou- pling coefficient gl(t). A phase difference φl between the two coupling points breaks the time-reversal symmetry. The total Hamiltonian is H = Ha + Hw + Haw (ℏ= 1),
